Sentiment Analysis

Wei-chen Huang
  • Side Projects
    • Web Scraping
      • NBA Scores
  • EPPS 6323
    • Assignments
      • Assignment 1

    • Project
      • Project Proposal

Sentiment Analysis

Goal: use various lexicons to assess the overall sentiment of various works of text.

library(janeaustenr)
library(stringr)
library(tidytext)
library(tidyverse)
── Attaching packages ─────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse 1.3.2 ──
✔ ggplot2 3.4.0     ✔ purrr   1.0.1
✔ tibble  3.1.8     ✔ dplyr   1.1.0
✔ tidyr   1.3.0     ✔ forcats 1.0.0
✔ readr   2.1.3     
── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
library(gutenbergr)
library(textdata)

original_books <- austen_books() %>%
  group_by(book) %>%
  mutate(linenumber = row_number(),
         chapter = cumsum(str_detect(text, 
                                     regex("^chapter [\\divxlc]",
                                           ignore_case = TRUE)))) %>%
  ungroup()

tidy_books <- original_books %>%
  unnest_tokens(word, text)
tidy_books
# A tibble: 725,055 × 4
   book                linenumber chapter word       
   <fct>                    <int>   <int> <chr>      
 1 Sense & Sensibility          1       0 sense      
 2 Sense & Sensibility          1       0 and        
 3 Sense & Sensibility          1       0 sensibility
 4 Sense & Sensibility          3       0 by         
 5 Sense & Sensibility          3       0 jane       
 6 Sense & Sensibility          3       0 austen     
 7 Sense & Sensibility          5       0 1811       
 8 Sense & Sensibility         10       1 chapter    
 9 Sense & Sensibility         10       1 1          
10 Sense & Sensibility         13       1 the        
# … with 725,045 more rows
data(stop_words)
tidy_books <- tidy_books %>%
  anti_join(stop_words, by = NULL)
Joining with `by = join_by(word)`
tidy_books %>%
  count(word, sort = TRUE) %>%
  filter( n > 600) %>%
  mutate(word = reorder(word, n)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(n, word)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(y = NULL)

oliver_twist <- gutenberg_download(c(730))
Determining mirror for Project Gutenberg from https://www.gutenberg.org/robot/harvest
Using mirror http://aleph.gutenberg.org
tidy_oliver <- oliver_twist %>%
  unnest_tokens(word, text)%>%
  anti_join(stop_words, by = NULL)
Joining with `by = join_by(word)`
tidy_oliver <- tidy_oliver %>%
  cbind(as.numeric(rownames(tidy_oliver)))
colnames(tidy_oliver)[3] <- "word_number"
tidy_oliver

nrc_surprise <- get_sentiments("nrc") %>%
  filter(sentiment == "surprise")
tidy_books %>%
  filter(book == "Emma") %>%
  inner_join(nrc_surprise) %>%
  count(word, sort = TRUE)
Joining with `by = join_by(word)`
# A tibble: 161 × 2
   word         n
   <chr>    <int>
 1 hope       143
 2 deal        92
 3 spirits     64
 4 marry       63
 5 leave       58
 6 feeling     56
 7 smile       44
 8 pleasant    41
 9 fortune     39
10 obliging    34
# … with 151 more rows
afinn_oliver <- tidy_oliver %>%
  inner_join(get_sentiments("afinn")) %>%
  group_by(index = word_number %/% 800) %>%
  summarize(sentiment = sum(value))%>%
  mutate(method = "AFINN")
Joining with `by = join_by(word)`
afinn_oliver %>%
  ggplot(aes(index, sentiment, fill = method)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~method, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y")

Looking at units beyond just words - other units of text can provide a better understanding of the sentiment as a whole.

library(janeaustenr)
library(stringr)
library(tidytext)
library(tidyverse)
library(gutenbergr)
library(textdata)
